does: This paper addresses the computation of robustness certifications for neural networks by introducing the apothem measure as an alternative to volume-based certification. The authors develop an algorithm that computes apothem-optimal certifications—axis-aligned hyper-rectangles around inputs that are guaranteed safe from adversarial perturbations—using a linear number of calls to a neural network verifier relative to the input domain's diameter. They prove fundamental intractability results showing that volume-optimal certifications cannot be computed in polynomial time via oracle-based approaches, establishing apothem-optimality as a computationally tractable objective. The work additionally introduces dual certifications that characterize all instances belonging to a particular class within a certified region.
connects: This work builds on the extensive literature on adversarial robustness and certification methods that emerged in response to adversarial examples in neural networks. It extends prior research on robustness certification techniques, which typically employ interval-based approaches and oracle calls to neural network verifiers. The paper responds directly to recent intractability results that show volume-optimal certifications are computationally prohibitive, proposing apothem-optimality as a more tractable alternative. This connects to broader research on formal verification of neural networks and the theoretical foundations of certified robustness, advancing the understanding of what certification objectives are computationally feasible given oracle-based computational constraints.
enables: This work enables more practical and trustworthy robustness certification pipelines for deployed neural networks by establishing apothem-optimality as a computationally efficient certification objective. The theoretical intractability results it proves guide future research away from volume-optimal approaches toward geometrically motivated alternatives. The introduction of dual certifications opens new directions for understanding decision boundaries and providing class-level guarantees rather than instance-level protection. These advances support the development of AI safety toolkits that can provide genuine, computationally verifiable robustness guarantees, potentially informing standards for certification in safety-critical applications and enabling practitioners to distinguish between certifications that are theoretically sound versus those that may provide false confidence.
